Report: Pre Professional Practice  
Faculty of Engineering and Applied Sciences  
Los Andes University 
  
Clara Leighton Parot  
Computer Science Engineering 
  
Enaex S.A 
Explosives manufacturing and blasting solutions for mining 
 
  
Abstract  
Enaex S.A. is a global leader in explosives manufacturing and blasting solutions for the mining 
industry. Operating in over 40 countries, the company focuses on innovation, safety, and efficiency 
to optimize blasting processes. One of its key projects is the development of wireless primers, an 
alternative to wired detonation systems. 
During my pre-professional practice in the Innovation and Marketing Department, I contributed to 
an extension of the wireless primer system. My task was to develop a communication extension 
between the Programming Unit and the Blast Driver to increase the operational range. To achieve 
this, I implemented a distributed system using two Raspberry Pi devices communicating via LoRa. 
Additionally, I developed a custom transmission protocol to handle data synchronization and error 
management. 
Key challenges included hardware integration, protocol synchronization, and system debugging in a 
multi-device environment. I also had to adapt to hardware restrictions, simulate missing 
components, and work under strict deadlines, as the project was already in a commercial phase. 
This experience strengthened my technical skills, problem-solving abilities, and ability to work under 
pressure. I had to learn programming formalities that have to be met in the field. It also gave me 
insight into real-world engineering, how different it is to approach a project when it has a real 
application. It marked a turning point in my abilities as an engineer and expanded my 
understanding of where I can apply my degree. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 ​
 
 
 

 
1.​Introduction  
1.1​Company Description  
Enaex S.A. is a Chilean company and a global leader in the production and commercialization of 
explosives and blasting solutions for the mining industry. Founded in 1920, the company has 
expanded its operations internationally, with a presence in over 40 countries and production centers 
in America, Europe, Africa, and Oceania. It specializes in the manufacturing of Porous Prill 
Ammonium Nitrate (ANPP), a key component in industrial explosives, and provides blasting and 
rock fragmentation services for large-scale mining operations. 
Currently, Enaex employs more than 7,089 people and has maintained steady growth thanks to its 
focus on innovation, automation, and safety. Its impact on the mining industry is reflected in its 
strong financial performance, achieving an annual net profit of USD 153.694 million and an EBITDA 
of USD 326.676 million in 2023. Additionally, the company was recognized with the "Círculo Da Vinci 
2023" award, ranking among the top 10 most innovative companies worldwide in the Most 
Innovative Companies ranking. 
Enaex is structured to ensure smooth coordination between its different areas, allowing for effective 
collaboration across innovation, production, and commercialization. The organizational chart below 
provides an overview of how the company is structured. 
​
Diagram 1.1: Enaex Organizational Chart 2023 
 
 
1.2​Department/Area Description  
Within the company structure, the Vice Presidency of Innovation and Marketing, led by Pablo 
Wallach Beovic, plays a key role in the development of new mining technologies, ensuring that 
technical solutions align with the company's commercial and strategic objectives.​
​
During my internship, I was part of the Innovation and Marketing Department, a key area in Enaex 
that focuses on developing new technologies to make mining safer, more efficient, and more 
modern. This department includes different teams that work on research, product innovation, and 
commercialization. Within the area, there are specialists in electrical engineering, robotics, software 
 

 
development, project management, marketing, and sales, all working together to create and launch 
new technologies. In the diagram down below you can see the main areas that contribute to this 
department and my position in them.  
 
 
Diagram 1.2: Enaex Marketing and Innovation Department 
Throughout my internship, I worked under the supervision of Sebastián Cerda Díaz, Technology 
Manager, mainly with a team focused on developing a wireless detonation system (Wireless Primer). 
This was one of the many projects led by Sebastián. The goal of this project was to replace 
traditional wired blasting systems in mining, removing the need for long cables while improving 
safety and efficiency in the blasting process.​
​
Additionally, the Innovation and Marketing Department works closely with other areas of the 
company, such as finances, production, and field operations, to make sure that every new 
technology developed meets the needs of both the market and the clients. This collaboration 
helped me understand the connection between innovation, technology development, and product 
commercialization, giving me a clearer view of how projects impact the company. 
 
 
 

 
2.​Work Details  
2.1​General Activities  
In the mining industry, one of the most critical processes is blasting operations, where detonators 
are used to fragment rock and facilitate the extraction of minerals. Traditionally, these detonators 
are connected through long-distance cables, which presents several challenges, such as complex 
installation, risk of cable damage during drilling, and limited operational flexibility. To overcome 
these limitations, Enaex is developing a Wireless Primer system, a wireless electronic initiation 
method designed to eliminate the dependence on physical wiring. This project introduces significant 
technological challenges, particularly in communication stability, detonator synchronization, and 
remote control of blasting operations, which are crucial for its successful implementation. 
The Wireless Primer system consists of three main components: the detonator, the HUB, and the 
operator. The detonator is responsible for executing the explosion after receiving the activation 
signal. The HUB manages communication with the detonators, ensuring proper synchronization and 
safe execution of the blasting process. Finally, the operator is the one who sends the activation 
command to the HUB, triggering the detonation process. 
​
 
Image 2.1: Initial DaveyTronic System Diagram 
I was integrated into the development team to implement an extension to the Wireless Primer 
system, increasing the control distance between the operator and the HUB to enhance safety in 
mining operations. To achieve this, I implemented a distributed system using two Raspberry Pi 
devices as intermediary nodes, enabling efficient and reliable communication between the operator 
and the detonation system. 
 
Imagen 2.2: Diagrama Sistema DaveyTronic  con Extensión de Comunicación 
The new system extends the communication architecture between the operator and the Blast Driver 
(DBD). Previously, the Programming Unit (PU) sent commands directly to the DBD, but now they 
first pass through Raspberry Pi 1 (RPI1), which was configured as an Access Point (AP) to enable 
wireless connection with the PU. To achieve this, it was necessary to set up and automate the AP 
on RPI1 using a system service that ensures its activation when powering up the device. 
Once the command is received, a Flask web server on RPI1 processes it and serializes the data for 
transmission. The message is then sent to Raspberry Pi 2 (RPI2) using LoRa through serial 
 

 
communication. Both Raspberry Pi devices communicate exclusively via LoRa, which required 
programming and configuring these modules. Serialization converts the data into a format suitable 
for serial transmission. Each message is encoded in Base64 and assigned a unique CRC to verify its 
integrity. RPI2 receives the message, and checks their integrity using CRC. Then, the RPI2, which is 
connected to the Blast Driver (DBD) via WiFi, sends the request to be executed. To make this work, 
a system service was developed to scan WiFi networks, find a specific pattern, and automatically 
connect to the DBD, ensuring stable communication without needing manual intervention. 
Once the Blast Driver processes the request and generates a response, it is sent back to RPI2, 
which serializes it again and transmits it to RPI1 through LoRa. RPI1 then decodes the response 
and forwards it to the PU, confirming to the operator that the command was executed successfully. 
The development of the Wireless Primer system at Enaex involves a team of experts from different 
areas and locations, each responsible for a specific part of the system. My role mainly focused on 
establishing communication between the HUB and the PU. I worked on this part of the project 
independently, with support from my supervisor when needed, but without additional team 
members directly involved. Since the project was divided into different areas and international 
teams, communication between groups was not always frequent, so my interaction with them was 
limited. The team includes people mainly from the United States, Brazil, Canada and France.  
One important factor was that this was a product already in the commercialization stage, meaning 
that I had to frequently interact with the sales team. There were active bidding processes that 
depended on the success of every part of the system, which added extra pressure to make sure 
that my progress met the technical and commercial requirements within the deadlines. 
Additionally, I often assisted my supervisor in testing the communication between the HUB and the 
detonators. This required going to field tests to check the signal range and stability in blasting 
operations, ensuring the system worked correctly under real operating conditions. The following 
images show part of this experience, where I not only supported my supervisor but also worked on 
my own project in the field, using the equipment available for testing. 
Image 2.3: Field Testing of Detonation System 
Image 2.4: Field Testing of Communication Extension Project 
Image 2.5: Soldering and Assembly of Components for Detonators 
 
 

 
2.2​Most Challenging Activity  
The development of this project presented several technical and management challenges, requiring 
not only software skills but also intensive learning in hardware, simulation, and organizing a 
distributed system. Throughout the process, I encountered multiple obstacles that forced me to 
adapt, learn quickly, and design effective solutions. Next, I will describe the four most challenging 
activities I faced during my practice. The first was the biggest technical challenge, related to 
synchronization in LoRa communication, which required the most effort in programming, analysis, 
and problem-solving. The other three were general challenges that I had to deal with throughout 
the project's development. 
2.2.1 LoRa Communication 
During the implementation of the system, I encountered two main challenges related to data 
transmission through LoRa. 
2.2.1.1 LoRa Challenge 1: Limitation in the Number of Characters Transmitted 
The first issue I encountered was that LoRa modules do not allow sending messages of any size. 
Initially, when testing the communication, I noticed that some messages were being cut off, and 
only part of the data was being received on the other end. After running multiple tests, I identified 
a consistent pattern in the number of characters being transmitted before the message was cut. 
This led me to review the LoRa module documentation, where I discovered that there was a 
restriction on the maximum number of characters that could be sent in a single transmission. This 
meant that long messages could not be sent directly. 
To solve this, I designed a method to split the data into smaller packets, where each fragment 
complied with LoRa’s transmission limits and could be sent independently. Each packet included a 
unique CRC, allowing the receiver to verify its integrity once received. Additionally, the final packet 
included the CRC of the original message, ensuring that once all packets were received and 
connected, the system could verify the integrity of the complete message before processing it. 
However, implementing this system led to a more difficult challenge: ensuring that the receiver had 
enough time to process each packet before the next one was sent, preventing data loss. 
2.2.1.2 LoRa Challenge 2: Synchronization 
To solve this challenge, I designed and implemented a communication protocol that ensured safe 
data transmission. This protocol needed to synchronize the transmission of multiple packets, 
prevent data loss, and guarantee that the message was correctly reconstructed by the receiver. 
However, while developing the protocol, I encountered a critical issue: LoRa modules are not 
bidirectional, meaning they cannot send and receive data simultaneously. Although the protocol 
allowed data transmission in packets, this limitation made synchronization between the sender and 
receiver even more hard. 
The main issue was that if the sender transmitted a packet while the receiver was still in 
transmission mode or not ready to receive, the message would be lost. To solve this, I had to 
design a synchronization protocol to ensure that each module was in the correct state at the right 
time before continuing transmission. 
​
​
 
 

 
The final protocol worked as follows: 
1.​ Communication Start: Before sending any data, the sender informs the receiver of the total 
number of packets to be transmitted. A message with the format "PACKETS:X" is sent, 
where X represents the total number of packets. 
2.​ Receiver Confirmation: Once the receiver gets this information it responds with "READY" 
indicating that it is prepared to receive data, and switches to RX mode. 
3.​ Packet Transmission and Verification: 
a.​ The sender sends a packet and switches to RX mode to wait for confirmation. 
b.​ The receiver, upon receiving the packet correctly, switches to TX mode and 
responds with "NEXT", indicating that it is ready for the next packet. 
c.​ If a packet is not received correctly, the receiver can request a retransmission. 
4.​ Finalization and Message Reconstruction: Once the last packet has been sent and 
confirmed, the receiver reconstructs the original message and verifies its integrity using 
CRC. 
 
Image 2.6: Graphic representation of communication protocol 
Because LoRa does not support simultaneous bidirectional communication, this protocol was an 
even more complex challenge, as it had to ensure that each module was in the correct state, 
reading or writing, at the exact moment required. If synchronization failed, the transmission would 
break, causing data loss or errors in the communication. 
This protocol not only ensured synchronization between the LoRa modules but also allowed for 
efficient error handling during transmission. Thanks to its implementation, I was able to establish a 
stable, secure, and reliable communication system between the Raspberry Pi devices, ensuring that 
each message was transmitted and received without errors within the extended Wireless Primer 
system. 
2.2.2 Hardware Handling and Multiple Components 
From the start, I had to learn how to properly use the Raspberry Pi pins, understand how to power 
the devices correctly, and make sure that each component was connected without causing damage. 
One of my biggest mistakes was incorrectly powering a Raspberry Pi, which ended up burning the 
device. This experience made me take hardware documentation more seriously, leading me to 
carefully research voltage requirements and connection setups before testing hardware 
components. 
 

 
I also had to develop new hardware skills to progress in the project. I learned to work with 
protoboards, properly connect components without damaging them, and in some cases, find 
creative solutions when resources were not available. For example, when I needed to make a 
connection but did not have proper cables, I ended up stripping wires using a kitchen knife from 
the office to keep working. Additionally, I learned how to solder to create permanent connections in 
the detonators. 
A key aspect was learning to diagnose whether an issue was software or hardware related. Many 
times, the system did not work as expected, and I had to analyze whether the problem was in the 
code, the device configuration, or an electrical connection failure. Developing the ability to 
distinguish between these types of errors was essential to avoid wasting time on unnecessary 
debugging and to optimize time and effort. 
2.2.3 Adapting to Equipment Unavailability 
This was a very complex project, where I had to manage multiple devices and each system involved 
in the communication. Below is a graphical representation of what my workstation usually looked 
like. As you can see, it involved many devices and components, so, as expected, sometimes various 
issues and obstacles arose, preventing me from having access to the full system at all times.​
​
 
Image 2.7: Workstation Setup for System Development and Testing 
​
Since this was a complex distributed system, I often had limited access to key devices, which forced 
me to constantly adapt. Many times, I could not use the HUB because it was being tested in the 
field or reconfigured by another team. Other times, I did not have a second Raspberry Pi, which 
made it difficult to simulate the complete system. Occasionally, I also lacked access to the PU 
control device, preventing the complete end to end testing. 
To overcome these issues, I had to develop alternative strategies to simulate or replace unavailable 
devices. One solution was to replicate the HUB on my computer, allowing me to test communication 
without needing the physical device. However, this introduced new challenges because my 
computer was already simulating a Raspberry Pi. Being able to simulate the HUB on my computer 
meant not having a second Raspberry Pi available. 
The best solution was to get a second Raspberry Pi and an additional screen, allowing me to 
distribute simulations more efficiently. However, many times I did not have enough screens or 
computers available, so I even had to temporarily modify my code to adapt to the devices I had at 
the time. I adjusted configurations to test the system in limited environments, while ensuring that 
everything could return to normal when all devices were available. 
Another major issue was not having the PU control device, which prevented me from testing real 
requests sent to the system. To solve this, I learned to use Postman, a tool that allowed me to 
 

 
manually replicate the necessary HTTP requests. This let me validate communication without the 
physical device, ensuring that the system was functioning correctly. 
This challenge taught me the importance of flexibility and adaptability in development 
environments. I learned to find alternative solutions when the necessary devices were unavailable 
and also learned new tools like Postman. The ability to work with limited resources was essential to 
keep making progress and avoid being completely blocked when equipment was not accessible. 
2.2.4 Managing a Distributed System​
​
Lastly, but definitely not least, one of the biggest challenges I faced in this project was learning 
how to properly structure and organize a distributed system. At first, I focused on programming the 
different components separately without considering how they would integrate. However, as the 
project progressed, I realized that I had built a disorganized system and I didnt have a clear vision 
in the way the whole system worked together, and the clear steps through the communication 
process. After weeks of work, I found myself in a situation that forced me to redo certain parts 
from scratch. 
Beyond system design issues, another major challenge was identifying the source of errors within 
the system. Since I was working with multiple interconnected devices, when something failed, it 
was not always easy to determine where the issue originated. For example, I spent two weeks 
convinced that there was an error in the communication between RPI1 and RPI2, when in reality, 
the issue was caused by a misconfiguration in the PU. This experience taught me that I could not 
immediately assume the cause of a failure, but instead needed to follow a structured approach to 
rule out possible issues rather than wasting time fixing things that were not actually broken. 
To overcome these problems, I had to change my approach. I learned to test each system 
component separately before integrating them, ensuring that they functioned correctly before 
connecting everything. I also started documenting my changes and tests more carefully which 
helped me avoid repeating the same mistakes. Most importantly, I realized that before writing any 
code, I needed to have a clear system design. Making significant changes after weeks of work was 
far more difficult than planning everything properly from the beginning. 
This challenge taught me that developing a system is not just about programming and making it 
work, it also requires order, planning, and a structured approach. 
​
 
 

 
3.​Analysis  
3.1​Company Analysis  
Enaex has established itself as a leader in the mining industry by focusing on innovation, safety, 
and operational efficiency. Over the years, the company has evolved from being a supplier of 
explosives to becoming a key player in integrated blasting solutions, with a strong commitment to 
digital transformation and automation. This approach has enabled Enaex to not only improve its 
production capacity but also to provide safer and more cost-effective solutions for the mining sector. 
From a productivity and operational capacity standpoint, Enaex has continued to expand, 
positioning itself as a global benchmark in the sector. With an annual nitrate ammonium production 
of 794 thousand tons and a total production capacity of 850 thousand tons per year, the company 
demonstrates its strong presence in the market. In financial terms, Enaex has maintained steady 
growth, achieving a net income of 153.694 MUS$ and an EBITDA of 326.676 MUS$ in 2023, which 
has allowed it to continue investing in technological advancements and global expansion. 
​
Table 3.1: Key Indicators of Enaex in 2023 
Beyond productivity and financial performance, Enaex stands out for its strong corporate culture 
and core values. Enaex’s vision of "Humanizing Mining" and its goal of becoming the preferred 
partner for premium blasting solutions demonstrate its long-term commitment to innovation and 
social responsibility.The company’s core values define its approach to work and guide its decision 
making. In 2023, Enaex established four corporate values, which are shown below.  
​
  
Image 3.1: Enex corporate values 
 

 
I worked at the Innovation and Marketing department, and I got to see first hand the impact 
innovation has on production and innovation. Enaex does not only focus on explosive production 
but has built an ecosystem of innovation that allows it to stand out in the market. The company’s 
digitalization and automation strategies have led to the implementation of electronic detonation 
systems, remote monitoring, and real-time data analysis, improving efficiency and safety in mining 
operations. They are constantly looking to improve the mining world, from its technologies to the 
relationships with their customers. They are always looking one step ahead, looking for things to 
improve, searching for new technologies, and interested in any feedback they could get. ​
​
Beyond these fundamental principles, one of the most remarkable aspects of Enaex’s work 
environment is the respectful and welcoming culture among employees. Every morning, employees 
personally greet each colleague on the floor, regardless of the number of people present, this 
creates an inclusive and friendly atmosphere. This routine reinforces a strong sense of teamwork 
and collaboration. Additionally, punctuality and respect for time are highly valued, ensuring 
meetings and deadlines are met efficiently, which strengthens the company’s professional and 
organized work environment. This is by far the biggest shock I had working there, it was truly 
amazing the respect and friendliness everybody that worked there had, and you can tell just by 
being there a single day, that their work environment is truly based in the values they claim to 
follow.  
When I first arrived at the company, Enaex for me was this incredible high-tech and innovative 
company, with impressive numbers and a reputation that had earned the respect of not only the 
mining industry but many others as well. After working there, even for just two months, I now see 
Enaex as a much more complete company, built on trust, security, respect, passion, and genuine 
care for its customers. I was lucky enough to attend several meetings where I got to learn about 
other projects my department was involved in. One that stood out the most was Enaex Bright, a 
project that managed to digitize the entire mining process and data, allowing clients to have 
complete transparency and real-time access to their business operations, no matter what. Seeing 
the level of dedication behind these projects made me realize that Enaex isn’t just about 
technology, it’s about creating solutions that truly matter. That is what Enaex is: excellence, 
innovation, and care. 
3.2​Work Analysis  
3.2.1 My work and contribution 
One of Enaex’s most important innovation projects is the Wireless Primer, a system designed to 
eliminate the need for physical wiring in blasting operations. The company identified that traditional 
wired detonation systems had several issues, such as complex installation, high maintenance costs, 
and safety risks. The Wireless Primer project was created to solve these problems, allowing 
electronic initiation without using physical cables. 
However, for the system to be fully functional and safe in a real mining environment, it needed a 
secure and reliable way to communicate between the operator and the HUB. A key part of this 
project was increasing the distance between the HUB and the operator, allowing remote control of 
the detonation process. This is where my work became important. I was responsible for developing 
the extension that enabled long distance communication between the operator and the HUB, 
making sure data was sent safely and with no errors. 
To achieve this, I implemented a system using Raspberry Pi that allowed wireless communication 
between the operator and the HUB. By integrating LoRa and WiFi, the system made sure that 
detonation commands were transmitted securely, even in difficult mining conditions. This extension 
 

 
was essential for the practical use of the Wireless Primer, because without it, the system would not 
have been safe or reliable enough for real mining operations. 
3.2.2 Mistakes, lessons, and future measures 
During my work, I made several mistakes that could have been prevented with better planning and 
preparation. These errors slowed me down at times, but they also helped me learn and improve. 
One of the biggest mistakes I made was not handling pressure well. When deadlines were tight and 
unexpected problems appeared, I sometimes felt blocked and unsure of how to continue. Instead 
of taking a step back to analyze the problem calmly, I got overwhelmed by the stress of the 
situation. To avoid this in the future, I could divide the work into smaller tasks so that I always have 
a clear next step instead of feeling lost. ​
​
Another important mistake was not reading the hardware documentation properly before using it. 
This led to wasted time and even damaged hardware. For example, I burned a Raspberry Pi 
because I connected it to the wrong voltage. If I had read the documentation carefully, I could 
have avoided this issue completely. I also spent a lot of time trying to figure things out on my own, 
when in reality, the documentation already had the answers. In future projects, I will make sure to 
review the technical manuals and specifications first so that I don’t repeat this mistake. 
A third mistake, and by far the biggest, was not designing the full system before starting to 
program. My mind was full of ideas, and I got excited about the project, so I rushed into coding 
without planning the architecture properly. This led to several revisions and delays, because I had 
to go back and fix things that I hadn’t thought about before. If I had taken more time to design the 
system and define how everything should connect, I would have been able to work more efficiently 
and focus on improving details instead of fixing major issues. Although this mistake made me very 
frustrated at the time, before this project I wasn't even familiarized with the concept of high level 
system design. If I had never run into issues of this nature, I wouldn't have been forced to learn to 
design and structure a system properly, so as frustrating as it was, I am glad it happened, because 
it meant a huge turning point in the way I view programming.  
 
​
 
​
​
​
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
​
 
 

 
4.​Personal Reflection  
I never knew what I wanted to study. I had some ideas, but engineering was never in my range of 
possibilities. When the time came to choose and apply, I was still just as lost, so I decided to go for 
the most unknown and intimidating option: engineering. I entered the program without a clear 
direction or goal, unsure of what to expect. As I progressed in my studies, I realized that I loved 
everything about engineering. I found myself fascinated by all its fields and applications. 
Engineering is not an easy path, but for me, the most difficult part was not the coursework, but 
having to choose a specialization. I enjoyed every aspect of engineering, making it incredibly hard 
to commit to just one. I ultimately chose Computer Engineering because I believed it would allow 
me to apply my knowledge across multiple fields without being restricted to one discipline. 
However, there were times when I doubted my decision. While my classmates were deeply engaged 
in the technical aspects of computing, I often found myself looking at other disciplines, fascinated 
by their applications. 
That is precisely why I chose Enaex for my internship. Enaex represents how Computer Engineers 
have a place and are needed even in fields traditionally dominated by other specializations, such as 
mining. During this experience, I learned that I had not made a mistake in choosing Computer 
Engineering, on the contrary, this field allows me to develop skills that are essential in every 
engineering discipline. My perspective expanded, and I was amazed by the vast opportunities my 
career offers.  
Professionally, I grew in ways I never expected. I learned to take ownership of my mistakes, fix 
them efficiently, and work independently. My approach to programming changed entirely. I now 
write code with a more mature and structured mindset, understanding that engineering is not just 
about solving problems, but about building reliable solutions that impact real projects that are much 
more complex. This experience showed me that applying engineering in the real world requires 
considering many more factors than just writing functional code. It gave me a new perspective on 
my career, showing me how my skills can be applied beyond the classroom and into real-world 
challenges. 
One of the most valuable lessons I gained from this experience was realizing that I have the skills. 
At the start of my internship, I was surrounded by highly experienced professionals, and I couldn't 
help but feel like I wasn’t good enough, like maybe I wasn’t cut out for this field. However, as time 
passed, I built confidence in my abilities and learned that with effort and dedication, I do have a 
place in this industry.  
For me now, being a Computer Science Engineer means having the ability to adapt and contribute 
to any field. Unlike other specializations that follow a more defined path, Computer Science allows 
me to integrate my knowledge into different industries without being limited to a single discipline. I 
can always find a way to apply my skills and bring value. This flexibility makes me feel like I don’t 
have to choose just one direction, I can explore, learn, and evolve across multiple areas. Through 
this experience, I realized how powerful that is and how many opportunities my degree truly offers, 
it's like a superpower! I finished my practice more eager than ever to keep learning and growing as 
an engineer and I will continue to choose the unknown and the intimidating.

Enaex is structured to ensure smooth coordination between its different areas, allowing for effective

Throughout my internship, I worked under the supervision of Sebastián Cerda Díaz, Technology

general_activities.texto:
General Activities  
In the mining industry, one of the most critical processes is blasting operations, where detonators 
are used to fragment rock and facilitate the extraction of minerals. Traditionally, these detonators 
are connected through long-distance cables, which presents several challenges, such as complex 
installation, risk of cable damage during drilling, and limited operational flexibility. To overcome 
these limitations, Enaex is developing a Wireless Primer system, a wireless electronic initiation 
method designed to eliminate the dependence on physical wiring. This project introduces significant 
technological challenges, particularly in communication stability, detonator synchronization, and 
remote control of blasting operations, which are crucial for its successful implementation. 
The Wireless Primer system consists of three main components: the detonator, the HUB, and the 
operator. The detonator is responsible for executing the explosion after receiving the activation 
signal. The HUB manages communication with the detonators, ensuring proper synchronization and 
safe execution of the blasting process. Finally, the operator is the one who sends the activation 
command to the HUB, triggering the detonation process. 
​
 
Image 2.1: Initial DaveyTronic System Diagram 
I was integrated into the development team to implement an extension to the Wireless Primer 
system, increasing the control distance between the operator and the HUB to enhance safety in 
mining operations. To achieve this, I implemented a distributed system using two Raspberry Pi 
devices as intermediary nodes, enabling efficient and reliable communication between the operator 
and the detonation system. 
 
Imagen 2.2: Diagrama Sistema DaveyTronic  con Extensión de Comunicación 
The new system extends the communication architecture between the operator and the Blast Driver 
(DBD). Previously, the Programming Unit (PU) sent commands directly to the DBD, but now they 
first pass through Raspberry Pi 1 (RPI1), which was configured as an Access Point (AP) to enable 
wireless connection with the PU. To achieve this, it was necessary to set up and automate the AP 
on RPI1 using a system service that ensures its activation when powering up the device. 
Once the command is received, a Flask web server on RPI1 processes it and serializes the data for 
transmission. The message is then sent to Raspberry Pi 2 (RPI2) using LoRa through serial 
 

 
communication. Both Raspberry Pi devices communicate exclusively via LoRa, which required 
programming and configuring these modules. Serialization converts the data into a format suitable 
for serial transmission. Each message is encoded in Base64 and assigned a unique CRC to verify its 
integrity. RPI2 receives the message, and checks their integrity using CRC. Then, the RPI2, which is 
connected to the Blast Driver (DBD) via WiFi, sends the request to be executed. To make this work, 
a system service was developed to scan WiFi networks, find a specific pattern, and automatically 
connect to the DBD, ensuring stable communication without needing manual intervention. 
Once the Blast Driver processes the request and generates a response, it is sent back to RPI2, 
which serializes it again and transmits it to RPI1 through LoRa. RPI1 then decodes the response 
and forwards it to the PU, confirming to the operator that the command was executed successfully. 
The development of the Wireless Primer system at Enaex involves a team of experts from different 
areas and locations, each responsible for a specific part of the system. My role mainly focused on 
establishing communication between the HUB and the PU. I worked on this part of the project 
independently, with support from my supervisor when needed, but without additional team 
members directly involved. Since the project was divided into different areas and international 
teams, communication between groups was not always frequent, so my interaction with them was 
limited. The team includes people mainly from the United States, Brazil, Canada and France.  
One important factor was that this was a product already in the commercialization stage, meaning 
that I had to frequently interact with the sales team. There were active bidding processes that 
depended on the success of every part of the system, which added extra pressure to make sure 
that my progress met the technical and commercial requirements within the deadlines. 
Additionally, I often assisted my supervisor in testing the communication between the HUB and the 
detonators. This required going to field tests to check the signal range and stability in blasting 
operations, ensuring the system worked correctly under real operating conditions. The following 
images show part of this experience, where I not only supported my supervisor but also worked on 
my own project in the field, using the equipment available for testing. 
Image 2.3: Field Testing of Detonation System 
Image 2.4: Field Testing of Communication Extension Project 
Image 2.5: Soldering and Assembly of Components for Detonators

most_challenge_activity.texto:
Most Challenging Activity  
The development of this project presented several technical and management challenges, requiring 
not only software skills but also intensive learning in hardware, simulation, and organizing a 
distributed system. Throughout the process, I encountered multiple obstacles that forced me to 
adapt, learn quickly, and design effective solutions. Next, I will describe the four most challenging 
activities I faced during my practice. The first was the biggest technical challenge, related to 
synchronization in LoRa communication, which required the most effort in programming, analysis, 
and problem-solving. The other three were general challenges that I had to deal with throughout 
the project's development. 
2.2.1 LoRa Communication 
During the implementation of the system, I encountered two main challenges related to data 
transmission through LoRa. 
2.2.1.1 LoRa Challenge 1: Limitation in the Number of Characters Transmitted 
The first issue I encountered was that LoRa modules do not allow sending messages of any size. 
Initially, when testing the communication, I noticed that some messages were being cut off, and 
only part of the data was being received on the other end. After running multiple tests, I identified 
a consistent pattern in the number of characters being transmitted before the message was cut. 
This led me to review the LoRa module documentation, where I discovered that there was a 
restriction on the maximum number of characters that could be sent in a single transmission. This 
meant that long messages could not be sent directly. 
To solve this, I designed a method to split the data into smaller packets, where each fragment 
complied with LoRa’s transmission limits and could be sent independently. Each packet included a 
unique CRC, allowing the receiver to verify its integrity once received. Additionally, the final packet 
included the CRC of the original message, ensuring that once all packets were received and 
connected, the system could verify the integrity of the complete message before processing it. 
However, implementing this system led to a more difficult challenge: ensuring that the receiver had 
enough time to process each packet before the next one was sent, preventing data loss. 
2.2.1.2 LoRa Challenge 2: Synchronization 
To solve this challenge, I designed and implemented a communication protocol that ensured safe 
data transmission. This protocol needed to synchronize the transmission of multiple packets, 
prevent data loss, and guarantee that the message was correctly reconstructed by the receiver. 
However, while developing the protocol, I encountered a critical issue: LoRa modules are not 
bidirectional, meaning they cannot send and receive data simultaneously. Although the protocol 
allowed data transmission in packets, this limitation made synchronization between the sender and 
receiver even more hard. 
The main issue was that if the sender transmitted a packet while the receiver was still in 
transmission mode or not ready to receive, the message would be lost. To solve this, I had to 
design a synchronization protocol to ensure that each module was in the correct state at the right 
time before continuing transmission. 
​
​
 
 

 
The final protocol worked as follows: 
1.​ Communication Start: Before sending any data, the sender informs the receiver of the total 
number of packets to be transmitted. A message with the format "PACKETS:X" is sent, 
where X represents the total number of packets. 
2.​ Receiver Confirmation: Once the receiver gets this information it responds with "READY" 
indicating that it is prepared to receive data, and switches to RX mode. 
3.​ Packet Transmission and Verification: 
a.​ The sender sends a packet and switches to RX mode to wait for confirmation. 
b.​ The receiver, upon receiving the packet correctly, switches to TX mode and 
responds with "NEXT", indicating that it is ready for the next packet. 
c.​ If a packet is not received correctly, the receiver can request a retransmission. 
4.​ Finalization and Message Reconstruction: Once the last packet has been sent and 
confirmed, the receiver reconstructs the original message and verifies its integrity using 
CRC. 
 
Image 2.6: Graphic representation of communication protocol 
Because LoRa does not support simultaneous bidirectional communication, this protocol was an 
even more complex challenge, as it had to ensure that each module was in the correct state, 
reading or writing, at the exact moment required. If synchronization failed, the transmission would 
break, causing data loss or errors in the communication. 
This protocol not only ensured synchronization between the LoRa modules but also allowed for 
efficient error handling during transmission. Thanks to its implementation, I was able to establish a 
stable, secure, and reliable communication system between the Raspberry Pi devices, ensuring that 
each message was transmitted and received without errors within the extended Wireless Primer 
system. 
2.2.2 Hardware Handling and Multiple Components 
From the start, I had to learn how to properly use the Raspberry Pi pins, understand how to power 
the devices correctly, and make sure that each component was connected without causing damage. 
One of my biggest mistakes was incorrectly powering a Raspberry Pi, which ended up burning the 
device. This experience made me take hardware documentation more seriously, leading me to 
carefully research voltage requirements and connection setups before testing hardware 
components. 
 

 
I also had to develop new hardware skills to progress in the project. I learned to work with 
protoboards, properly connect components without damaging them, and in some cases, find 
creative solutions when resources were not available. For example, when I needed to make a 
connection but did not have proper cables, I ended up stripping wires using a kitchen knife from 
the office to keep working. Additionally, I learned how to solder to create permanent connections in 
the detonators. 
A key aspect was learning to diagnose whether an issue was software or hardware related. Many 
times, the system did not work as expected, and I had to analyze whether the problem was in the 
code, the device configuration, or an electrical connection failure. Developing the ability to 
distinguish between these types of errors was essential to avoid wasting time on unnecessary 
debugging and to optimize time and effort. 
2.2.3 Adapting to Equipment Unavailability 
This was a very complex project, where I had to manage multiple devices and each system involved 
in the communication. Below is a graphical representation of what my workstation usually looked 
like. As you can see, it involved many devices and components, so, as expected, sometimes various 
issues and obstacles arose, preventing me from having access to the full system at all times.​
​
 
Image 2.7: Workstation Setup for System Development and Testing 
​
Since this was a complex distributed system, I often had limited access to key devices, which forced 
me to constantly adapt. Many times, I could not use the HUB because it was being tested in the 
field or reconfigured by another team. Other times, I did not have a second Raspberry Pi, which 
made it difficult to simulate the complete system. Occasionally, I also lacked access to the PU 
control device, preventing the complete end to end testing. 
To overcome these issues, I had to develop alternative strategies to simulate or replace unavailable 
devices. One solution was to replicate the HUB on my computer, allowing me to test communication 
without needing the physical device. However, this introduced new challenges because my 
computer was already simulating a Raspberry Pi. Being able to simulate the HUB on my computer 
meant not having a second Raspberry Pi available. 
The best solution was to get a second Raspberry Pi and an additional screen, allowing me to 
distribute simulations more efficiently. However, many times I did not have enough screens or 
computers available, so I even had to temporarily modify my code to adapt to the devices I had at 
the time. I adjusted configurations to test the system in limited environments, while ensuring that 
everything could return to normal when all devices were available. 
Another major issue was not having the PU control device, which prevented me from testing real 
requests sent to the system. To solve this, I learned to use Postman, a tool that allowed me to 
 

 
manually replicate the necessary HTTP requests. This let me validate communication without the 
physical device, ensuring that the system was functioning correctly. 
This challenge taught me the importance of flexibility and adaptability in development 
environments. I learned to find alternative solutions when the necessary devices were unavailable 
and also learned new tools like Postman. The ability to work with limited resources was essential to 
keep making progress and avoid being completely blocked when equipment was not accessible. 
2.2.4 Managing a Distributed System​
​
Lastly, but definitely not least, one of the biggest challenges I faced in this project was learning 
how to properly structure and organize a distributed system. At first, I focused on programming the 
different components separately without considering how they would integrate. However, as the 
project progressed, I realized that I had built a disorganized system and I didnt have a clear vision 
in the way the whole system worked together, and the clear steps through the communication 
process. After weeks of work, I found myself in a situation that forced me to redo certain parts 
from scratch. 
Beyond system design issues, another major challenge was identifying the source of errors within 
the system. Since I was working with multiple interconnected devices, when something failed, it 
was not always easy to determine where the issue originated. For example, I spent two weeks 
convinced that there was an error in the communication between RPI1 and RPI2, when in reality, 
the issue was caused by a misconfiguration in the PU. This experience taught me that I could not 
immediately assume the cause of a failure, but instead needed to follow a structured approach to 
rule out possible issues rather than wasting time fixing things that were not actually broken. 
To overcome these problems, I had to change my approach. I learned to test each system 
component separately before integrating them, ensuring that they functioned correctly before 
connecting everything. I also started documenting my changes and tests more carefully which 
helped me avoid repeating the same mistakes. Most importantly, I realized that before writing any 
code, I needed to have a clear system design. Making significant changes after weeks of work was 
far more difficult than planning everything properly from the beginning. 
This challenge taught me that developing a system is not just about programming and making it 
work, it also requires order, planning, and a structured approach. 
​
 
 

 
3.​Analysis  
3.1​

company_analysis.texto:
Company Analysis  
Enaex has established itself as a leader in the mining industry by focusing on innovation, safety, 
and operational efficiency. Over the years, the company has evolved from being a supplier of 
explosives to becoming a key player in integrated blasting solutions, with a strong commitment to 
digital transformation and automation. This approach has enabled Enaex to not only improve its 
production capacity but also to provide safer and more cost-effective solutions for the mining sector. 
From a productivity and operational capacity standpoint, Enaex has continued to expand, 
positioning itself as a global benchmark in the sector. With an annual nitrate ammonium production 
of 794 thousand tons and a total production capacity of 850 thousand tons per year, the company 
demonstrates its strong presence in the market. In financial terms, Enaex has maintained steady 
growth, achieving a net income of 153.694 MUS$ and an EBITDA of 326.676 MUS$ in 2023, which 
has allowed it to continue investing in technological advancements and global expansion. 
​
Table 3.1: Key Indicators of Enaex in 2023 
Beyond productivity and financial performance, Enaex stands out for its strong corporate culture 
and core values. Enaex’s vision of "Humanizing Mining" and its goal of becoming the preferred 
partner for premium blasting solutions demonstrate its long-term commitment to innovation and 
social responsibility.The company’s core values define its approach to work and guide its decision 
making. In 2023, Enaex established four corporate values, which are shown below.  
​
  
Image 3.1: Enex corporate values 
 

 
I worked at the Innovation and Marketing department, and I got to see first hand the impact 
innovation has on production and innovation. Enaex does not only focus on explosive production 
but has built an ecosystem of innovation that allows it to stand out in the market. The company’s 
digitalization and automation strategies have led to the implementation of electronic detonation 
systems, remote monitoring, and real-time data analysis, improving efficiency and safety in mining 
operations. They are constantly looking to improve the mining world, from its technologies to the 
relationships with their customers. They are always looking one step ahead, looking for things to 
improve, searching for new technologies, and interested in any feedback they could get. ​
​
Beyond these fundamental principles, one of the most remarkable aspects of Enaex’s work 
environment is the respectful and welcoming culture among employees. Every morning, employees 
personally greet each colleague on the floor, regardless of the number of people present, this 
creates an inclusive and friendly atmosphere. This routine reinforces a strong sense of teamwork 
and collaboration. Additionally, punctuality and respect for time are highly valued, ensuring 
meetings and deadlines are met efficiently, which strengthens the company’s professional and 
organized work environment. This is by far the biggest shock I had working there, it was truly 
amazing the respect and friendliness everybody that worked there had, and you can tell just by 
being there a single day, that their work environment is truly based in the values they claim to 
follow.  
When I first arrived at the company, Enaex for me was this incredible high-tech and innovative 
company, with impressive numbers and a reputation that had earned the respect of not only the 
mining industry but many others as well. After working there, even for just two months, I now see 
Enaex as a much more complete company, built on trust, security, respect, passion, and genuine 
care for its customers. I was lucky enough to attend several meetings where I got to learn about 
other projects my department was involved in. One that stood out the most was Enaex Bright, a 
project that managed to digitize the entire mining process and data, allowing clients to have 
complete transparency and real-time access to their business operations, no matter what. Seeing 
the level of dedication behind these projects made me realize that Enaex isn’t just about 
technology, it’s about creating solutions that truly matter. That is what Enaex is: excellence, 
innovation, and care. 
3.2​

work_analysis.texto:
Work Analysis  
3.2.1 My work and contribution 
One of Enaex’s most important innovation projects is the Wireless Primer, a system designed to 
eliminate the need for physical wiring in blasting operations. The company identified that traditional 
wired detonation systems had several issues, such as complex installation, high maintenance costs, 
and safety risks. The Wireless Primer project was created to solve these problems, allowing 
electronic initiation without using physical cables. 
However, for the system to be fully functional and safe in a real mining environment, it needed a 
secure and reliable way to communicate between the operator and the HUB. A key part of this 
project was increasing the distance between the HUB and the operator, allowing remote control of 
the detonation process. This is where my work became important. I was responsible for developing 
the extension that enabled long distance communication between the operator and the HUB, 
making sure data was sent safely and with no errors. 
To achieve this, I implemented a system using Raspberry Pi that allowed wireless communication 
between the operator and the HUB. By integrating LoRa and WiFi, the system made sure that 
detonation commands were transmitted securely, even in difficult mining conditions. This extension 
 

 
was essential for the practical use of the Wireless Primer, because without it, the system would not 
have been safe or reliable enough for real mining operations. 
3.2.2 Mistakes, lessons, and future measures 
During my work, I made several mistakes that could have been prevented with better planning and 
preparation. These errors slowed me down at times, but they also helped me learn and improve. 
One of the biggest mistakes I made was not handling pressure well. When deadlines were tight and 
unexpected problems appeared, I sometimes felt blocked and unsure of how to continue. Instead 
of taking a step back to analyze the problem calmly, I got overwhelmed by the stress of the 
situation. To avoid this in the future, I could divide the work into smaller tasks so that I always have 
a clear next step instead of feeling lost. ​
​
Another important mistake was not reading the hardware documentation properly before using it. 
This led to wasted time and even damaged hardware. For example, I burned a Raspberry Pi 
because I connected it to the wrong voltage. If I had read the documentation carefully, I could 
have avoided this issue completely. I also spent a lot of time trying to figure things out on my own, 
when in reality, the documentation already had the answers. In future projects, I will make sure to 
review the technical manuals and specifications first so that I don’t repeat this mistake. 
A third mistake, and by far the biggest, was not designing the full system before starting to 
program. My mind was full of ideas, and I got excited about the project, so I rushed into coding 
without planning the architecture properly. This led to several revisions and delays, because I had 
to go back and fix things that I hadn’t thought about before. If I had taken more time to design the 
system and define how everything should connect, I would have been able to work more efficiently 
and focus on improving details instead of fixing major issues. Although this mistake made me very 
frustrated at the time, before this project I wasn't even familiarized with the concept of high level 
system design. If I had never run into issues of this nature, I wouldn't have been forced to learn to 
design and structure a system properly, so as frustrating as it was, I am glad it happened, because 
it meant a huge turning point in the way I view programming.  
 
​
 
​
​
​
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
​
 
 

 
4.​

personal_reflection.texto:
Personal Reflection  
I never knew what I wanted to study. I had some ideas, but engineering was never in my range of 
possibilities. When the time came to choose and apply, I was still just as lost, so I decided to go for 
the most unknown and intimidating option: engineering. I entered the program without a clear 
direction or goal, unsure of what to expect. As I progressed in my studies, I realized that I loved 
everything about engineering. I found myself fascinated by all its fields and applications. 
Engineering is not an easy path, but for me, the most difficult part was not the coursework, but 
having to choose a specialization. I enjoyed every aspect of engineering, making it incredibly hard 
to commit to just one. I ultimately chose Computer Engineering because I believed it would allow 
me to apply my knowledge across multiple fields without being restricted to one discipline. 
However, there were times when I doubted my decision. While my classmates were deeply engaged 
in the technical aspects of computing, I often found myself looking at other disciplines, fascinated 
by their applications. 
That is precisely why I chose Enaex for my internship. Enaex represents how Computer Engineers 
have a place and are needed even in fields traditionally dominated by other specializations, such as 
mining. During this experience, I learned that I had not made a mistake in choosing Computer 
Engineering, on the contrary, this field allows me to develop skills that are essential in every 
engineering discipline. My perspective expanded, and I was amazed by the vast opportunities my 
career offers.  
Professionally, I grew in ways I never expected. I learned to take ownership of my mistakes, fix 
them efficiently, and work independently. My approach to programming changed entirely. I now 
write code with a more mature and structured mindset, understanding that engineering is not just 
about solving problems, but about building reliable solutions that impact real projects that are much 
more complex. This experience showed me that applying engineering in the real world requires 
considering many more factors than just writing functional code. It gave me a new perspective on 
my career, showing me how my skills can be applied beyond the classroom and into real-world 
challenges. 
One of the most valuable lessons I gained from this experience was realizing that I have the skills. 
At the start of my internship, I was surrounded by highly experienced professionals, and I couldn't 
help but feel like I wasn’t good enough, like maybe I wasn’t cut out for this field. However, as time 
passed, I built confidence in my abilities and learned that with effort and dedication, I do have a 
place in this industry.  
For me now, being a Computer Science Engineer means having the ability to adapt and contribute 
to any field. Unlike other specializations that follow a more defined path, Computer Science allows 
me to integrate my knowledge into different industries without being limited to a single discipline. I 
can always find a way to apply my skills and bring value. This flexibility makes me feel like I don’t 
have to choose just one direction, I can explore, learn, and evolve across multiple areas. Through 
this experience, I realized how powerful that is and how many opportunities my degree truly offers, 
it's like a superpower! I finished my practice more eager than ever to keep learning and growing as 
an engineer and I will continue to choose the unknown and the intimidating.
